parentQuestion: A patient develops sudden paralysis and loss of all sensation below the waist after an abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. Pain and temperature sensation return partially after a few days, but motor function, proprioception, and vibration sense remain absent. This pattern suggests ___.

scaffolds.q: Does the spinal cord need blood supply?
options: Yes, it needs blood for oxygen and nutrients like any organ
scaffolds.explain: The spinal cord needs OXYGEN and NUTRIENTS from blood. If blood supply is cut off (a stroke of the spinal cord), the affected tissue can die. This is a SPINAL CORD INFARCT.

wrongExplains.0: All neural tissue requires blood supply. Without it, cells die.
wrongExplains.2: Both brain AND spinal cord need continuous blood supply.
wrongExplains.3: Blood is essential. Loss of blood supply causes infarction.

scaffolds.q: How does blood reach the spinal cord?
options: Through arteries that run along its length
The spinal cord makes its own blood
scaffolds.explain: Three main ARTERIES supply the spinal cord: One ANTERIOR spinal artery (front) and two POSTERIOR spinal arteries (back). These run vertically along the cord.

wrongExplains.0: Blood comes from arteries, not muscles. Muscles receive blood.
wrongExplains.2: Blood is made in bone marrow. The cord receives blood through arteries.
wrongExplains.3: Skin doesn't supply the cord. Arteries penetrate to reach the cord.

scaffolds.q: What does the ANTERIOR spinal artery supply?
options: Only the back of the cord
The FRONT portion (about two-thirds) of the spinal cord
scaffolds.explain: The ANTERIOR spinal artery supplies about the front TWO-THIRDS of the cord. This includes motor pathways (corticospinal tracts) and the spinothalamic tract (pain/temperature).

wrongExplains.0: The back is supplied by POSTERIOR arteries. Anterior supplies the front.
wrongExplains.2: The anterior spinal artery runs along the spinal cord, not the brain.
wrongExplains.3: It supplies a critical portion of the cord — motor and pain pathways.

scaffolds.q: What do the POSTERIOR spinal arteries supply?
options: The BACK portion (about one-third) of the spinal cord
scaffolds.explain: The POSTERIOR spinal arteries (two of them) supply the back ONE-THIRD of the cord. This includes the DORSAL COLUMNS (proprioception and vibration).

wrongExplains.0: The front is supplied by the ANTERIOR artery. Posterior supplies the back.
wrongExplains.2: Muscles have their own blood supply. Posterior arteries supply the cord.
wrongExplains.3: The brainstem has different blood supply. Posterior spinal arteries supply the cord.

scaffolds.q: Which pathways are supplied by the anterior spinal artery?
scaffolds.explain: The anterior cord contains: CORTICOSPINAL TRACTS (motor), SPINOTHALAMIC TRACTS (pain/temp), and anterior horn motor neurons. All supplied by the ANTERIOR spinal artery.

wrongExplains.0: Dorsal columns are POSTERIOR — supplied by posterior arteries.
wrongExplains.2: Very important pathways are here — motor and pain sensation.
wrongExplains.3: Neural pathways, not just blood vessels, depend on this artery.

scaffolds.q: Which pathway is supplied by the posterior spinal arteries?
scaffolds.explain: The DORSAL COLUMNS sit at the back of the cord and are supplied by the POSTERIOR spinal arteries. If only the anterior artery is blocked, dorsal columns are SPARED.

wrongExplains.0: Motor pathways are anterior. Posterior arteries supply dorsal columns.
wrongExplains.2: Pain/temp (spinothalamic) is anterior. Posterior supplies proprioception.
wrongExplains.3: Each artery supplies a specific region — not all pathways.

scaffolds.q: How can aortic surgery damage the spinal cord?
options: It cannot affect the spinal cord
The anterior spinal artery gets blood from aortic branches that may be damaged
The spinal cord is in the aorta
scaffolds.explain: The anterior spinal artery gets blood from arteries branching off the AORTA. During aortic surgery (like aneurysm repair), these feeding arteries may be damaged, causing SPINAL CORD ISCHEMIA.

wrongExplains.0: Aortic surgery is a known risk for spinal cord ischemia.
wrongExplains.2: Surgery can inadvertently damage blood supply to the cord.
wrongExplains.3: The spinal cord is in the vertebral canal, but its blood supply connects to the aorta.

scaffolds.q: What does anterior spinal artery syndrome cause?
options: Motor paralysis and loss of pain/temp, but proprioception is PRESERVED
scaffolds.explain: When the anterior artery is blocked: ANTERIOR CORD dies (motor and pain/temp pathways), but POSTERIOR CORD survives (dorsal columns preserved). Patient can still sense position but can't move or feel pain.

wrongExplains.0: Proprioception is actually PRESERVED because posterior arteries supply dorsal columns.
wrongExplains.2: Spinal cord infarction causes permanent deficits, not recovery.
wrongExplains.3: Sensation is lost (pain/temp) or diminished, not enhanced.

scaffolds.q: Why might pain sensation partially recover before motor function?
options: Spinothalamic tract is more resilient than motor neurons to ischemia
They recover at exactly the same rate
scaffolds.explain: Motor neurons in the anterior horn are very VULNERABLE to ischemia and die quickly. The spinothalamic tract fibers may partially recover as the ischemic penumbra improves. This creates a tragic pattern: Patients regain ability to FEEL PAIN but remain paralyzed.

wrongExplains.0: Pain sensation is real neural function, not imaginary.
wrongExplains.2: Motor neurons are actually more vulnerable and may not recover.
wrongExplains.3: Different tissues have different vulnerabilities to ischemia.
